topic: patient story — footballer refusing to do his home exercises

turns.content: I need to vent about a patient because I am going to lose my mind haha
Go for it — sometimes you just need to get it out. What's going on with this patient?

okay so I have this footballer, he plays semi-pro, he had a grade 2 hamstring strain about six weeks ago. And I gave him a very clear, very structured home exercise program — we're talking 15 minutes a day, nothing crazy. Eccentric hamstring curls, hip bridges, some mobility work. He comes in for his sessions, does everything I ask him, great form, seems engaged. And then I find out he is doing NOTHING at home. Zero. Nada.
That's incredibly frustrating, especially when the in-clinic effort makes you think they're invested. How did you find out he wasn't doing the home program? And did he give a reason for skipping it?

I asked him to show me his hip bridge progression because last week I gave him a single-leg variation and he looked at me like I was speaking another language! He had no idea what I was talking about. So I said okay be honest with me, are you doing the exercises at home? And he goes "nah not really, I just come here and you fix me" — AI MEU DEUS. "You fix me." Like I'm a mechanic and he's a car haha

Oh man, the "you fix me" mentality is so common and so frustrating for therapists. He's basically treating his sessions like a car wash — drop in, get serviced, leave unchanged. The reality is that in-clinic sessions are maybe 2-3 hours a week at most, and the other 165 hours are where the actual recovery happens. Has he set a return-to-play target date? Sometimes tying the home program to a concrete deadline — like missing the start of next season — can shift the motivation.

that's exactly what I tried! His team starts preseason in about five weeks and I told him straight up — if you don't do the home program, you will not be ready. The eccentric strengthening is what protects him from re-injury, and I can't do that work FOR him. He said "yeah yeah okay" but gente I have seen that face before. That's the face of a person who is not going to do it

You're probably right about that face — compliance with home exercise programs is notoriously low across the board, something like 50-65% of patients don't follow through. A few strategies that sometimes break through: have him film himself doing the exercises and send you the videos. It creates accountability without extra visits. Also, simplify the program even further — if 15 minutes feels like too much, give him the absolute non-negotiable minimum, maybe just the eccentric curls. Getting him to do one exercise consistently is better than a perfect program he ignores.

the video idea is actually really smart! Because then he can't lie to me haha. And you're right about simplifying — I always struggle with that because I KNOW what the ideal program looks like, but ideal means nothing if he won't do it. Maybe I reduce it to literally three exercises, five minutes. If he can't give me five minutes a day for his own career then I don't know what to tell him honestly

Exactly — the best program is the one the patient actually does. And for a competitive athlete, you can also try reframing it away from "rehab" language entirely. Instead of "home exercises for your injury," position them as "performance training to come back faster and stronger than before." Athletes often resist anything that sounds like rehab because it makes them feel broken, but they'll do extra training all day long. The psychology of the framing matters a lot.

ohhh I like that reframe a lot!! You're so right — the word "rehab" has this energy of weakness for athletes, but if I say this is your prehab protocol to come back stronger, it's a totally different conversation. Nossa why didn't I think of that?? Okay I'm going to try this approach next session. Three exercises, filmed, framed as performance work. If this doesn't work then I truly give up haha

I think that combination — fewer exercises, video accountability, and the performance framing — is going to land a lot better than what you had before. And if it still doesn't work, at least you'll know you tried every angle and it's genuinely on him at that point. Some patients need to learn the hard way by missing the preseason deadline. Hopefully it doesn't come to that though.

I really hope not because he's talented and I hate seeing people sabotage their own recovery! But you're right, I can only do so much. Okay I feel better after this vent session haha. Thank you querida!

Glad you got it off your chest! Good luck with the next session — I hope the reframing does the trick. And for what it's worth, the fact that you care this much about his outcome says a lot about you as a therapist.

haha obrigada meu bem!! You know what, I'm going to walk into that next session with a whole new energy. Performance training, video check-ins, three exercises max. If this doesn't work then I'm printing a giant sign that says 'YOUR BODY IS NOT A CAR' and hanging it in my treatment room haha
I would honestly love to see that sign — it might be the most effective patient education tool ever created. Go in with confidence, you've got a solid game plan. Let me know how it goes!
